coredata.dc:title: Microwave abetted synthesis of carbon dots and its triple mode applications in tartrazine detection, manganese ion sensing and fluorescent ink
coredata.dc:creator: Korah, Binila K.
Thara, Chinnu R.
John, Neenamol
John, Bony K.
Mathew, Sneha
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are the emerging class of carbon-based nanomaterials with immense applications in various fields. In this work, CDs were synthesized using a simple microwave method using citric acid and tris base as precursors. The highly photoluminescent CDs were well characterized for examining the structural and optical properties. It was found successful for triple mode applications in food, metal ion sensing and fluorescent ink. As an inner filter-based fluorescent sensor, CDs detected tartrazine with an LOD (limit of detection) of 54 nM in the linear range 2–12 μM. The practicability of the probe was confirmed using its analysis in food samples. It also worked well as an electrochemical sensor for detecting Mn(II) ions in real water samples with a very low LOD of 0.37 nM. Finally, CDs were employed as a fluorescent ink for drawing and writing applications. High fluorescence of the as prepared CDs thus proved its potential for anticounterfeiting applications. This work not only adds new knowledge and data on material properties through thorough characterization of a new CD but also introduces a new sensor that is capable of triple mode application with excellent selectivity, sensitivity and practical viability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensing
Electrochemical sensing
Tartrazine
Manganese ions
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of tartrazine via fluorescence technique
Detection of manganese ions via electrochemical technique
Fluorescence analysis of tartrazine in food samples
Electrochemical analysis of Mn(II) in water samples
Fluorescence sensing mechanism of tartrazine detection
Fluorescence analysis of tartrazine in food samples
Electrochemical properties of CDs towards Mn (II) detection
Electrochemical sensing of Mn(II) ions and its mechanism
Electrochemical analysis of Mn (II) in water samples
CDs as fluorescent ink for anticounterfeiting applications

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thanks to Mahatma Gandhi university, Kottayam, Kerala for providing University Junior Research Fellowship and DST SAIF for providing the instrumentation facility for Raman and lifetime analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectrum, inset shows the color upon UV irradiation color, (b) fluorescence spectra (excitation and emission); (c) emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths, and (d) FT-IR of carbon dots.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CDs, and high-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.
(a) TEM (HRTEM in inset), (b) particle size distribution histogram, and (c) DLS spectrum of CDs.
(a) XRD spectrum, (b) raman spectrum, and (c) zeta potential curve of CDs.
Effect of (a) light irradiation, (b) storage time, (c) pH, and (d) ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

(a) Influence of tartrazine concentration on the PL intensity of CDs, (b) linear relation between PL intensity and various tartrazine concentrations, and (c) selectivity study of tartrazine in the presence of other interferents.
(a) Cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE, CD/GCE, and CD/GCE/Mn(II) in PBS, (b) effect of scan rate, (c) EIS spectra of CD/GCE, and (d) effect of storage time.

(a) Selectivity study with other metal ions, (b) Differential Pulse Voltammogram of CD/GCE with Mn(II) concentrations (10–70 nM), (c) plot of concentration of Mn(II) against peak current, (d) Interference study of CDs with (1) blank, (2) Pb(II), (3) Zn(II), (4) Cd(II), (5) Cr(II), (6) Hg(II), (7) Cu(II), (8) Ni(II), (9) Fe(II), and (3) Co(II) ions.
Fluorescent images of patterns, drawings and writings drawn using the synthesized CDs.
Synthesis methodology for the preparation of carbon dots from citric acid (CA) and tris base (TB).
Electrochemical mechanism of Mn(II) sensing using CDs.
Comparative analysis of fluorescence sensing of tartrazine using CDs with the existing reports.
Real sample analysis for tartrazine sensing using CDs.
Comparative analysis of the electrochemical sensing of Mn(II) ions using CDs with the existing reports.
Real sample analysis for Mn(II) sensing using CDs.

head.ce:title: Microwave abetted synthesis of carbon dots and its triple mode applications in tartrazine detection, manganese ion sensing and fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam, 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam, 686 560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are the emerging class of carbon-based nanomaterials with immense applications in various fields. In this work, CDs were synthesized using a simple microwave method using citric acid and tris base as precursors. The highly photoluminescent CDs were well characterized for examining the structural and optical properties. It was found successful for triple mode applications in food, metal ion sensing and fluorescent ink. As an inner filter-based fluorescent sensor, CDs detected tartrazine with an LOD (limit of detection) of 54 nM in the linear range 2–12 μM. The practicability of the probe was confirmed using its analysis in food samples. It also worked well as an electrochemical sensor for detecting Mn(II) ions in real water samples with a very low LOD of 0.37 nM. Finally, CDs were employed as a fluorescent ink for drawing and writing applications. High fluorescence of the as prepared CDs thus proved its potential for anticounterfeiting applications. This work not only adds new knowledge and data on material properties through thorough characterization of a new CD but also introduces a new sensor that is capable of triple mode application with excellent selectivity, sensitivity and practical viability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have attached supplementary data in the submission process

ce:section.ce:para: Tartrazine is a lemon yellow-colored food color which is basically a water-soluble azo dye. It is used by itself to impart a yellow color or in combination with others to produce different colors. It is used as a major component in food items, cosmetic materials, and pharmaceutical products. It has restrictions in certain countries due to the harmful effects such as respiratory diseases, skin allergy and migraines (Berzas et al., 1999; Kapadia et al., 1998). Tartrazine has the potential to stop physiological human functions through binding induced complex formations. When tartrazine is administered in lower or larger levels, it causes biliary cirrhosis, neurodegeneration, mutagenicity, alterations in hepatic and renal factors (Amin et al., 2010; Tanaka, 2006). In response, the food sector should carefully govern tartrazine levels in foodstuff, necessitating an interest in the production of an appropriate tartrazine detecting method and detector. The limitations of various methods point out to the use of evolving fluorescence-based detection of tartrazine, which is simple and economical compared to other techniques (Culzoni et al., 2009; Sahraei et al., 2013; Song et al., 2014; Soponar et al., 2008).

Metal ions play a key role in various human and environmental aspects. Of these metal ions, the third most abundant one, manganese, is important in enzyme activation, fertilizer, ceramic and dietary supplement production (Rezaei, Ghiaci, & Sedaghat, 2008a, 2008b; Roushani, Saedi, Hamdi, & Dizajdizi, 2017a, 2017b; Welch et al., 2006). Despite manganese does have many beneficial important biological functions, it accumulates in the ecosystem and has detrimental impact on living beings over time, though at low quantities. Manganese is mostly absorbed by humans via diets such as cereals, eggs, almonds, and shellfish (Zaidan et al., 2013). Manganese in the water system also has a role in the production of oxide films in eroded pipelines. As a result, the flow of water is harmed because manganese, also in lower amounts than iron, is obnoxious in water (Colombo et al., 2018; Trueman et al., 2019). Excess manganese intake (≥0.3 ppm) in drinkable water can induce drowsiness, weakened limbs, and migraines, as well as Parkinson's disease, lung thrombosis, and asthma (Akinsanya et al., 2019; Peres et al., 2016). Therefore, it is high time to develop apt sensors for the detection of manganese for protecting human health. Although there are variety of techniques and sensors for detecting Mn(II) ions (Hu et al., 2014; Ma & Yeh, 2015; Meng et al., 2017; Narayanan & Han, 2017), there are only a few works on carbon dot-based sensors for the electrochemical sensing of Mn(II) ions.

Carbon dots (CDs) have evolved into a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials that could really supersede semiconductor quantum dots (Baker & Baker, 2010). CDs are spherical carbon - containing nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm that are enriched by sp2 carbon and other functionalities (Zhu et al., 2013). Their excellent photostability, high quantum yield, variable surface composition, and nontoxicity, along with good biocompatibility, enabled them to be used as biological imaging tools, nanocarriers, sensors, fluorescent ink and many more applications (Li, Kang, Liu, & Lee, 2012a, 2012b). Carbon dots as a sensor is a fast-growing topic of research due to the biocompatibility of the sensor. In the literature, CDs are frequently used as "raw" sensors. Even in a biological environment, various metal cations as well as smaller molecules or proteins in solution can be found using variously manufactured CDs. Commonly, when CDs are dissolved in a solution containing a particular analyte, their luminescence is quenched, allowing for the detection of the analyte with high sensitivity and, frequently, high selectivity. This is made possible by properly engineering the surface functional groups so that they only interact with a particular type of metal cation.

This study focuses on the synthesis of carbon dots, its characterizations and three main applications of the as synthesized CDs. Herein, CDs were synthesized from citric acid and tris base through a simple and economical microwave method. The blue fluorescent CDs, with good quantum yield were characterized to reveal its structural and optical properties. It was used as a fluorescence sensor for the tartrazine detection in food and water samples with good limit of detection (LOD). As an electrochemical sensor, it detected Mn(II) ions with a LOD around 0.37 nM. The potential of CDs for anticounterfeiting applications was also investigated.

There are different conceptions of this work that distinguish it from others. We synthesized CDs from two precursors, citric acid and tris base, and elaborately characterized them. We, thereby tried a new combination of precursors for the synthesis, which actually contributes firsthand information to the material research society. In terms of application innovation, the same CDs have been effectively used in three important domains with great selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, the practical feasibility of CDs as a sensor in the detection of tartrazine and manganese ions has been verified. To our knowledge, no reports of CDs being used for all three applications with the same precursors have been recently discovered.

Chemicals used include citric acid, tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (Tris), sodium chloride, sodium phosphate monobasic, sodium phosphate dibasic, sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid. For interference studies arginine, leucine, valine, phenylalanine, ascorbic acid, lysine, fast sulphone B, sunset yellow, tartrazine, alizarin red, xylenol orange and glucose were also used. Metal salts used were CdCl2.H2O, FeSO4.7H2O, HgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, K2Cr2O7, MnCl2.4H2O, CoCl2.6H2O, ZnSO4.7H2O, NiCl2.6H2O, and CuSO4.5H2O. All chemicals were obtained from Merck, India and were of analytical grade. Chemicals have been employed without any additional purification. All throughout the experiments, double distilled water has been used.

A Perkin Elmer-400 spectrometer was used to acquire the (Fourier Transform Infrared) FTIR spectra. UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan was used to perform UV–vis. spectral analysis. A Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer was used to record the photoluminescence spectra. The (X-ray Diffraction) XRD was measured using X'PERT-PRO X-ray spectrometer. To obtain (High Resolution- Transmission Electron Microscopy) HR-TEM images, a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope was used. A Horiba SZ-100 scientific nanoparticle analyzer was used to perform (Dynamic Light Scattering) DLS and zeta measurements. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis have been conducted using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB spectrometer. A three-electrode configuration with in Biologic SP 200 workstation was used to execute all electrochemical assays. A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) served as the working electrode, while an Ag/AgCl electrode served as the reference electrode and a Pt wire served as the counter electrode.

Carbon dots were synthesized using citric acid and tris base as precursors using microwave method (Scheme 1 ). In the typical procedure citric acid (6.3 g) and tris base (3.6 g) in an optimized ratio 2:1 was taken and stirred with distilled water (50 ml). The homogeneous mixture was heated in a microwave oven for 6 min (650 mW). After cooling to room temperature, the solution was sonicated for 15 min for attaining uniform dispersion. The brown colored solution obtained was stored at 4 °C for further use.

Fluorescent sensors need to be stable in order to assure validity and consistency during sensing applications. Thus, the fluorescence stability of CDs under various pH, ionic concentrations, long time photoirradiation, and long period storage was investigated. The fluorescence intensity at an excitation of 380 nm of CDs at different pH (3–12), NaCl concentration (0.2–1 M), time intervals of photoirradiation, and storage was recorded and analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tartrazine via fluorescence technique
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical procedure for the sensing of tartrazine based on fluorescence technique is as follows: Carbon dot solution (300 μl) was diluted to 3 ml and different concentrations of tartrazine were added. The corresponding fluorescence intensities were noted (F0 and F) and analyzed. Interference study was also done to ensure the selectivity of CDs towards tartrazine. At the same analyte concentration (20 μM), the effects of interfering substances such as food additives and amino acids on the fluorescence sensing ability of tartrazine was investigated. It was done by recording the fluorescence of CDs in the presence of both tartrazine and other interfering substances at the same concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of manganese ions via electrochemical technique
ce:section.ce:para: Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was chosen as the working electrode for electrochemical experiments. The solution containing CDs was dispersed on the surface of GCE and dried. Cyclic voltammograms of the bare electrode, CD-coated electrode (CD/GCE) and modified electrode in the presence of various metal ions were recorded. Different concentrations (10–70 nM) of Mn(II) ions were prepared to obtain the differential pulse voltammetry data. Cyclic voltammograms at different scan rates of CD/GCE/Mn(II) was analyzed. Electrochemical impedence spectra of bare GCE and CD-coated GCE was also compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of tartrazine in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Candy, soft drink, river water and tap water were chosen as test samples for tartrazine detection. The pre-treatment procedure for candy and soft drink is as follows: Test samples obtained from local market were dissolved in water and heated to 70 °C. After dilution it was filtered, centrifuged (10,000g (g is the relative centrifugal force) for 10 min) for further investigation. The water from the tap and the water from the river were both purified. and used directly without any pre-treatment. Fluorescence analysis was done using the test samples, by altering the analyte concentrations in the test solution containing carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of Mn(II) in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and river water samples were taken as test samples for Mn(II) detection. Both of them was purified using filtration membrane (0.45 μm) and centrifuged (20,000g for 10 min) prior to electrochemical analysis. Membrane filtration and centrifugation removes only larger impurities present in the water sources. Electrochemical measurements were done for the test samples which were spiked with different analyte concentrations.

The carbon dot solution was transferred into an ink pen-refill tube without any pretreatment. It has been used to write and draw various letters and designs on filter paper. It was subjected to UV rays after drying in the open air to create fluorescent pictures.

Several techniques were utilized for the characterization of carbon dots. The optical properties were studied using absorption, and photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra. Absorption peaks in the range of 200–400 nm was ascribed to the n-π* (an electron in unshared pair on a hetero atom is excited to π∗ antibonding orbital) and π-π* (transition from bonding π orbital to antibonding π* orbital) transition in CO and CC bonds, respectively (Fig. 1 a) (Li et al., 2012b, 2012c, 2015). Excitation and emission spectra are shown in Fig. 1b. PL excitation independent emission obtained exhibits a maximum at 440 nm, when excited at 380 nm (Fig. 1c). The quantum yield of blue fluorescent CDs was calculated to be 14.14 percent. FT-IR spectrum shows prominent peaks at 3350, 2945, 1717, 1661 and 1532 cm−1 corresponding to –OH, –CH, –CO, –CC and –C-N stretching of amide carbonyl, respectively (Fig. 1d). This reveals that all of these functional groups can be found on the surface of CDs (Huang et al., 2018; Purbia & Paria, 2016).

The chemical bonds present on CDs was further explored by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The major peaks at 284.0, 396.8, and 530.6 eV corresponds to the presence of C, N, and O elements, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The C1s peak at 284.0 eV in Fig. 2b can be deconvoluted into three peaks positioned at 284.6, 285.8, and 286.9 eV, respectively, corresponding to CC, C–N, and CO bonds. The 396.1 eV N1s peak can be deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 2c), namely 396.1 eV (C–N–C), 397.3 eV (N-(C)3), and 398.5 eV (N–H). The CO bond was ascribed to the peak at 530.6 eV in the O1s spectrum (Dager et al., 2019) (Fig. 2d).

Raman analysis was used to determine the degree of structural defects in the produced CDs. Two peaks, corresponding to the D and G bands, were found at about 1342 and 1562 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 4c). The D band (disordered) is connected to the vibrations of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms with dangling bonds on the termination plane of the carbon structure. The sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice are related to the G band (graphitic carbon) (Chen et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2014). To measure the amount of the disorder, the Raman intensity ratio was determined and found to be 0.84, demonstrating the dominance of amorphous carbon atoms with in CDs. This is in accordance with the results obtained from transmission electron microscopy and XRD analysis.

CDs should be tested for stability in a range of circumstances to determine their viability. After exposing CDs to UV light and preserving them for a long period, the intensity was evaluated. CDs maintained steady emission throughout a 60-min period of UV irradiation, demonstrating their remarkable photostability (Fig. 5 a). Over the course of two weeks of incubation, there was no obvious difference in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5b). This demonstrated the excellent photostability and storage stability of CDs. The PL (photoluminescence) intensity of CDs was tested at various ionic concentrations and pH values to assure their stability. At different pH levels, the fluorescence intensity was noted and it was found that there was only a minor difference in the intensities with maximum intensity in the basic pH compared to acidic pH (Fig. 5c). There was no considerable variation in the fluorescence intensity of CDs even when subjected to elevated ionic concentrations (Fig. 5d).

Parameters including pH and incubation time were tuned to get a superior fluorescence sensitivity to the tartrazine. The influence of pH and incubation time on the detection capability of the sensor was explored. At all pH conditions, tartrazine could be detected using the synthesized CDs. However, pH 3 was chosen as the optimum pH to conduct the detection to acquire the highest level of sensitivity. According to Fig. S3a, the fluorescence quenching was found to be at its highest at pH 3. This is explained by the fact that the enhanced structural stiffness of CDs at lower pH encourages more contact with the tartrazine molecules in the solution. As a result, more studies were carried out at the optimal pH of 3. The reaction time was also optimized in order to make sure that the reaction was accomplished. There were no noticeable changes in the F/F0 signals (F is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of tartrazine and F0 is the intensity in the absence of tartrazine) after a 5-min incubation period, as shown in Fig. S3b. This suggests that the reaction took place right after the analytes were introduced, and the data was collected immediately after that.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was used to identify tartrazine by monitoring the emission spectrum at 380 nm excitation. The intensity of the fluorescence was reduced as the tartrazine concentration was increased (Fig. 6 a). When tartrazine concentration was plotted against matching intensity (F/F0 ratio) in the range of 2–12 μM a linear graph was observed, with a LOD of 54 nM (Fig. 6b). The influence of interfering compounds such as food additives and amino acids on the selectivity was examined at the same concentration (20 μM) of analyte (Fig. 6c). The results reveal that, despite the presence of other competing chemicals, tartrazine can be recognized selectively (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing mechanism of tartrazine detection
ce:section.ce:para: The initially noticed sensing mechanism in the detection of tartrazine by carbon dots is fluorescence quenching. After several experiments, more examination on the quenching mechanism was accomplished. The possibility of IFE (Inner Filter Effect) or FRET (Förster resonance energy transfer) as a viable quenching mechanism was shown by a good spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of tartrazine and the emission spectrum of CDs (Fig. S5a). The spectral overlap is an indication that there is possibility of absorption of the emission of CDs by the analyte. Only after comparing the lifetimes of CD and CD-tartrazine complexes was an exact determination achievable. FRET is accompanied by a decrease in lifetime, whereas IFE has no such effect. The lifetime curves of CD and CD-tartrazine systems were almost coincidental as illustrated in Fig. S5b. This confirmed the mechanism as inner filter effect (Korah et al., 2022; Qian et al., 2019; Yuan et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2018). Our study detects tartrazine with a lower LOD than previous reports (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of tartrazine in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis procedure was used to detect the presence of tartrazine at a trace concentration in several food products. Table S1 represents the comparison of tap water and river employed for the real sample analysis. The physical characteristics including pH, conductivity, temperature, and turbidity were analyzed and found that both the water sources have considerable differences. The conductivity of both samples is higher than the typical range for drinking water (200–1000 μS/cm). Conductivity outside of this range is a sign that the water is contaminated (Ma et al., 2020, p. 2020). Surface water systems typically have a pH between 6.5 and 8.5 (Rahman et al., 2021; BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards), 2012). Due to changes in the levels of CO2, carbonate, and bicarbonate in water, the pH values varied (Acharya et al., 2008). After standardizing the equipment with known buffer solutions of pH 4 and pH 7, the pH measurements of the water samples were immediately taken. The pH of the tap water was within the acceptable range; however, the pH of the river water was lower. The turbidity of both water samples has also been studied. Turbidity is a measure of the degree to which the water loses its transparency due to the presence of suspended particles. According to the WHO (World Health Organization), drinking water turbidity should ideally be less than 1 NTU and should not exceed 5 NTU (Benneyworth et al., 2016). The high turbidity of river water is an obvious sign of pollution brought on by suspended particles. Basically, temperature matters because of how it affects other waste water characteristics. When compared to tap water, river water has the highest value of temperature. Table 2 shows the results for pre-treated foods and water samples spiked using known levels of standard tartrazine. Each of these findings suggest that the following methodology may be used to determine tartrazine in food products with relatively high accuracy. The effect of pH on the fluorescence sensing of tartrazine using CDs as a fluorescence sensor was optimized prior to real sample analysis. However, taking into account the possibility of multiple interactions in complex matrices, the fluorescence sensing of tartrazine in soft drinks and candies under various pH conditions were investigated. And the results are summarized in the supplementary information (Table S2). Satisfactory recovery rates with relative standard deviation values below 5 was obtained over all pH conditions (pH 3–12). This proves the efficiency of the sensor in comparison to the existing methods of detection of tartrazine. The sensor developed is simple, non-toxic and works efficiently in all pH conditions. The fluorescence intensities were recorded before and after the addition of tartrazine in fluorescence-based sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of CDs towards Mn (II) detection
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical activity of the sensor was analyzed using cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The enhanced peak current in the case of CD/GCE than bare GCE when scanned in the potential range 0.0–1.0 V confirmed the electrochemical ability of the sensor. Furthermore, in the presence of Mn (II) ions, two major peaks were observed (oxidation peak at 0.9 V and reduction peak at 0.6 V) (Fig. 7 a). EIS also ensured the increased conductance of CD/GCE than bare GCE (Fig. 7b). The impact of scan rate on kinetic parameters was also examined, and a linear response with increasing scan rate (20–160 mV/s) was identified (Fig. 7c). The sensor retains the electrochemical property even after storage for a long period of time (Fig. 7d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing of Mn(II) ions and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the sensor towards the detection of Mn(II) ions was analyzed using CV and it was found that major peaks were obtained for Mn(II) rather than other metal ions (Fig. 8 a). The effect of varying concentrations of Mn(II) ions (10–70 nM) was also investigated using DPV analysis (Fig. 8b). A linear graph was obtained when the concentration of Mn(II) was plotted against current values with an LOD of 0.37 nM (Fig. 8c). Interference studies confirmed that the sensor could detect Mn(II) ions selectively and sensitively, since there was negligible difference in the current values even in the presence of other metal ions (Fig. 8d). Because the highest current was obtained at a pH of 3, all of the experiments were done at this optimum pH (Fig. S6). There are only few reports on the electrochemical sensing of Mn(II) ions using carbon quantum dots.

Comparison of the peaks clearly depicted that oxidation part has higher peaks than the reduction part. This owes to the hydrogen evolution potential in which oxidation part peaks higher than reduction part (Gilbert et al., 2019; Hwang et al., 2008; Xu et al., 2021). Oxidation peak current was obtained at 0.9 V and reduction peak current was obtained at 0.6 V. The proposed mechanism for the detection of Mn(II) ions on the surface of CD/GCE is illustrated in Scheme 2 .
Table 3 summarizes a comparative research on the electrochemical sensing of Mn(II) using various sensors, demonstrating the robustness of the proposed sensor over those reported before.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of Mn (II) in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing of Mn(II) employing CD/GCE via DPV analysis in river and tap water samples was conducted to explore the performance of sensor in real water samples. The RSD was less than 3% in all trials, and the recovery rates observed are a convincing indication of the viability of sensor in real samples as it is in the acceptable range of recovery (70–120%). (Table 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as fluorescent ink for anticounterfeiting applications
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of CDs to work as a fluorescent ink was investigated. Without the use of additional chemicals or pretreatments, the sample solution of CDs was effectively used as a fluorescent ink. Under UV light irradiation, the handwritten text and drawings on the filter paper fluoresced bright blue (Fig. 9 ). The images were preserved for six months and analyzed to determine the fluorescence stability. After a long storage period, clear and consistent pictures were obtained. Its ability to produce exceptionally distinct, clean, and long-lasting fluorescent images suggested that it could be used instead of standard inks (Atchudan et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2017). Another key property of the proposed ink is that it is colorless and nearly invisible to the naked eye under daylight, paving the path for anti-counterfeiting and data encryption applications (Ma et al., 2019).

Carbon dots were prepared from citric acid and tris base using microwave method. Systematic characterization revealed the formation of CDs with spherical morphology, enriched surface functionalities, hydrophilicity and good quantum yield. CDs have been proven to be successful in three areas, namely fluorescent probe, electrochemical probe and as a fluorescent ink. Tartrazine was detected through an inner filter-based fluorescence quenching mechanism. With an LOD of 54 nM, and linear range of 2–12 μM, CDs shine as a fluorescent probe for tartrazine detection. In real life samples such as soft-drinks, candies and various water sources, the viability of the probe was successfully investigated. Manganese ions was also detected by carbon dot modified electrode via electron transfer mechanism based electrochemical method. The LOD was 0.37 nM and the proposed electrochemical probe was validated for application in real water samples. As a fluorescent ink, CDs was employed for drawing and writing applications, paving the way for anticounterfeiting applications. The suggested CDs have several benefits over earlier approaches, including ease of use, high environment protection, wide linear range, low limit of detection, high efficiency, outstanding sensitivity, and immediate response.

Binila K. Korah: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Chinnu R. Thara: Software, Data curation. Neenamol John: Visualization, Investigation. Bony K. John: Visualization, Investigation. Sneha Mathew: Visualization, Investigation. Beena Mathew: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks to Mahatma Gandhi university, Kottayam, Kerala for providing University Junior Research Fellowship and DST SAIF for providing the instrumentation facility for Raman and lifetime analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2023.109608.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Acharya et al., 2008
